ontology.foodex2_description: Enchiladas — composite dish of corn tortillas filled with meat and topped with chilli sauce
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for enchiladas as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage of composite ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.description: 1 plate (2–3 enchiladas with sauce and toppings)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of beef enchiladas is approximately 400 g, comprising two to three rolled corn tortillas filled with seasoned ground beef, topped with red enchilada sauce and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Enchilada with beef and cheese (SR Legacy ID 21340)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 21340 (enchilada with beef and cheese) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy. Energy 162.5 kcal/100g, protein 8.75 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g are consistent with the USDA reference range for beef-and-cheese enchiladas (USDA reports ~168 kcal, 9.0 g protein, 9.8 g fat, 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Minor differences reflect sauce and cheese proportion variation. Input values retained with medium confidence and documented as adapted.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef enchiladas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the analogous food 'corn tortilla' (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, adjusted downward slightly for the protein and fat co-ingestion effect from beef and cheese filling which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 40.0) / 100 = 20.0, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 10.0 × 4 = 40.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit beef enchiladas to a half-portion (200 g) and request low-sodium enchilada sauce and reduced cheese to keep single-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10 g/100 g with estimated GI ~50 (low); a 400 g serving provides 40 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 20, which is at the upper boundary of the moderate GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit the serving to 200–250 g, choose corn over flour tortillas, request sauce without added sugar, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5 g/100 g and trans fat 0.25 g/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers 18 g saturated fat and 1 g trans fat, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200 g, request reduced cheese, choose lean ground beef (≥90% lean), and avoid sour cream toppings to keep saturated fat intake within the recommended 7% of total daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; combined with saturated fat approaching 5 g/100 g and nightshade-based enchilada sauce.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with grilled chicken or black beans, reduce cheese quantity, and monitor tolerance to tomato- and chilli-based sauces which are nightshade derivatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef enchiladas are a classic Mexican baked dish consisting of corn tortillas rolled around a seasoned ground beef filling, then arranged in a baking dish, smothered in red enchilada sauce (a tomato-and-dried-chilli-based sauce), topped with shredded cheddar or Monterey Jack cheese, and baked until the cheese is melted and bubbling. The dish originates from Mexico and is widely served across North America and in Mexican restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on Tex-Mex and Mexican-fusion menus. The texture combines soft, sauce-soaked tortillas with a savoury, slightly spiced beef interior and a rich, tangy sauce. Per 100 g, beef enchiladas provide approximately 162.5 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 10.0 g fat (4.5 g saturated), and 10.0 g carbohydrate with 1.25 g dietary fibre and 300 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is ~50 (low), driven by corn tortillas as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, moderated by co-ingested protein and fat. Clinically, the high sodium content (300 mg/100 g) is the primary concern for hypertensive patients; saturated fat and trans fat are relevant for cardiovascular disease risk; and the moderate glycemic load (GL ~20 per 400 g serving) requires portion control for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (flour tortilla variants) or dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef enchiladas?
Are beef enchiladas good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in beef enchiladas?
Can I eat enchiladas with high blood pressure?
Are enchiladas high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21340 (enchilada with beef and cheese). Core macros are consistent within acceptable variance. GI is an analogous estimate from corn tortilla data in the University of Sydney GI Database. No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for beef enchiladas. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and the composite nature of the dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from 'BEEF ENCHLADAS' to canonical 'Beef Enchiladas'; preserved typo as alias.
Replaced forbidden grabfood source with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 21340 as primary nutrient source.
Added GI value (50, low) with analogous estimate from corn tortilla data in University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 20.0 for 400 g serving.
Added health_context for Type 2 Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease.
Renamed 'diabetes' condition key to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Removed 'Naan' from ethnic_tags as it is inconsistent with Mexican cuisine.
Set monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, and all unavailable micronutrients to -1.
Added ontology section with FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT gap note.
Populated aliases_by_language with English and romanized Spanish forms; verified flat aliases consistency.
Added natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and culinary context.
Added romanized_local Spanish name 'Enchiladas de res' in local_names.
Iteration 3 fix: Expanded notes field to individually document every -1 field with specific reason and source consulted, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
